title: Number of Greater Elements to Right

statement.markdown: Given an array of integers `arr`, return a new array `result` such that each element `result[i]` is equal to the number of elements in `arr` that are greater than `arr[i]` and appear to the right of `arr[i]` in the array.
statement.inputFormat:
The number of elements in the array
The elements of the array
statement.outputFormat: The number of greater elements to the right for each element

constraints.description: The size of the input array
The range of each element in the array

examples.explanation: For the element 3, there are 2 elements (4 and 5) to its right that are greater. For the element 1, there is 1 element (2) to its right that is greater. For the element 4, there is 1 element (5) to its right that is greater. For the elements 2 and 5, there are no greater elements to their right.

For the element 2, there is 1 element (3) to its right that is greater. For the element 1, there is 1 element (3) to its right that is greater. For the element 3, there are no greater elements to its right.

hints.text: Consider iterating through the array from right to left. This might help you keep track of the elements you've seen so far and compare them to the current element.
Think about using a data structure to store the elements you've seen to the right of the current element. This could help you efficiently count the number of greater elements.

The problem can be solved using a Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) or a data structure that allows efficient insertion and counting of elements. Consider how you can use this to keep track of the count of greater elements.

editorial.title: Unlocking Efficiency: A Tale of Two Approaches for Counting Greater Elements to the Right
editorial.intuition: The key observation here is that we only need to keep track of the maximum element seen so far from the right. This allows us to avoid comparing each element with all elements to its right, which would be inefficient.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve for each element, iterating over all elements to its right and counting those that are greater. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2), which is inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: By iterating from right to left and keeping track of the maximum element seen so far, we can solve the problem much more efficiently.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves a single pass through the array from right to left, maintaining a running maximum of elements seen so far. This approach is known to have a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be seen by noting that for any given element, the number of greater elements to its right is equal to the number of elements to its right that are greater than the maximum element to its right, plus the number of elements that are equal to the maximum element to its right but to the right of it. However, since we only count elements strictly greater, the solution accurately reflects the number of greater elements to the right for each element.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs, such as [5, 7, -24, 12, 10, 2, -12, 6, 1, 3] helps validate its correctness.
commonMistakes: Not recognizing the need to iterate from right to left to efficiently solve the problem.
Incorrectly assuming that a data structure like a balanced binary search tree is needed to solve the problem efficiently.
Failing to account for the space needed to store the result array.
Not considering the edge case where the input array is empty or contains a single element.
Incorrectly handling the case where there are duplicate maximum elements to the right of an element.
Not verifying the solution with test cases.
